title: The Ten-Minute Reclaim: Touched Out
subtitle: A short ritual for reclaiming your body after a day of constant physical demand
excerpt: A ten-minute ritual for the end of a day in which your body has been in constant demand from others. A bounded practice for returning your body to you before the evening ends.
sourceNotes: Written for homemade.education. The five-part ritual structure draws on the ceremony shape from MONEY Journal (Rebecca Page, 2025). The concept of being 'touched out' is widely discussed in maternal health writing.

practice.whenToUse: Use at the end of a day in which your body has been held, climbed on, needed, or touched continuously and you feel the need to reclaim it.

content.text: A ten-minute evening ritual for the end of a day when your body has been in constant demand. The practice creates a physical boundary between the day's demands and the evening, and returns your body to you in a small, deliberate way.

Find ten minutes alone, even if that means waiting until they are in bed. Go somewhere with a door. A bathroom is fine. The point is one room with no one in it except you. Close the door. Stand or sit. Place both hands flat on your own body somewhere: your thighs, your stomach, your chest. Hold them there for thirty seconds.
I am ready to release the claim this day has had on my body. I release it now. I release it now. I release it now.
I am ready to allow my body to belong to me again. I allow it now. I allow it now. I allow it now.

Spend five minutes doing one thing that is only for your body: a stretch on the floor, a hot shower, a cup of tea held with both hands, lying flat on the bed. No screen. Nothing required of you. Five minutes.
This body is mine. The day is done.
Say it once. Go on with the evening.
Written for homemade.education. The five-part ritual structure draws on the ceremony shape from MONEY Journal (Rebecca Page, 2025).
